Home/Plan

Plan Your Growth

Choose the cycle before collecting links.

The guide works best when a person starts with a goal, chooses the audience, uses existing directories first, prepares one reusable packet, tests matched channels, then adjusts based on what happened.

Mobile quick route

Pick a route

Choose the closest need, then open the directory page instead of reading through the full planning guide.

DirectoryFundingArts & CultureTemplates

Growth cycle

Each step changes the next one.

Your goal shapes your audience. The audience determines which directories, partners, and calendars matter. Those channels determine what assets you need. The response tells you what to adjust before you repeat the cycle.

1

Name the job

Decide whether you are trying to launch, promote, correct, partner, fund, or expand.

2

Choose the audience

Pick the people who should notice, visit, register, refer, attend, or share.

3

Start with directories

Use existing lists before building a contact list by hand.

4

Prepare one packet

Make a short blurb, image, date, place, contact route, and plain call to action.

5

Test matched channels

Use a small set of calendars, media outlets, partners, or public channels that fit the job.

6

Track and repeat

Record what happened, thank the channel, and reuse what worked.

Find directories and leads

Use by need

Start with the job, then choose the section.

Search the rows in this section.

NeedStart hereFirst action
Get listedNetworkUse existing directories first.
Post an eventAmplify + PostChoose calendar, venue, media, or notice path.
Find partnersRegionUse county hubs and referral nodes.
Prepare outreachTemplatesMake one packet, then adapt it.
Correct dataSubmitSend source link plus correction.

Promotion packet

Templates are time-savers, not the strategy.

The useful strategy is to prepare one clean packet: a short blurb, image, flyer, listing details, date, location, hours, contact route, and plain call to action. The templates help you adapt that packet for directories, calendars, media, partners, emails, and social posts.

Use copy-ready templates Find places to send them

Free and freemium tools

Build the packet with tools people already recognize.

Plan limits change. Treat these as popular online starting points and verify current free-tier limits before relying on them for a campaign.

Canva

Flyers, social posts, simple ads, posters, and presentation graphics.

Free/freemium design tool; check current plan limits.

Adobe Express

Alternate flyer, social, photo, and quick brand-asset creation.

Free/freemium design tool; check current plan limits.

Google Forms

Signup forms, intake forms, update requests, and simple surveys.

Useful when a program needs responses before a full website exists.

Google Business Profile

Search and Maps visibility, hours, photos, posts, and basic business listing control.

Free listing path for storefronts and service-area businesses.

Meta Business Suite

Facebook and Instagram post scheduling, messaging, insights, and ad setup.

Free business dashboard; paid ads remain optional.

HubSpot Email Marketing

Email newsletters, outreach campaigns, simple CRM-linked lists, and templates.

Free/freemium email tools; check send limits before launch.

Mailchimp

Beginner-friendly mailing lists, email campaigns, audience forms, and reporting.

Free/freemium email marketing; check current contact and send limits.

Brevo

Email campaigns and contact lists when a small team needs a simple sender.

Free/freemium email marketing; check daily and monthly limits.

Funding and support readiness

Visibility work can create proof for later asks.

Grantors, lenders, sponsors, and partner organizations usually need more than enthusiasm. Track where you posted, who shared, what response arrived, and which partner can confirm the public benefit before using outreach results in a funding conversation.

Find funding and support leads

Cross-promotion loop

Use the channel that worked, then make the relationship stronger.

Submit the item, share the published result, thank the channel owner, report useful response, and reuse the relationship when it fits again.

Next action

Choose the next practical route.

  • Search directories and support leads
  • Find event calendars, newsletters, visitor guides, and directory channels
  • Use copy-ready outreach templates
  • Submit a correction with a public source link

Worked examples

Use the same cycle for different real-world jobs.

These examples show how the planning cycle changes when the user is a storefront, service provider, artist, musician, event organizer, or nonprofit board member.

New storefront launch

  1. Confirm license, address, hours, contact route, and opening date.
  2. Claim or update Google Business Profile and local directory listings.
  3. Prepare a launch packet with photos, short description, offer, hours, and call to action.
  4. Submit only where the opening is relevant: chamber, city, tourism, local media, and calendar channels.
  5. Track calls, visits, search terms, referrals, and where people say they found you.

Existing business expanding across county lines

  1. Pick one outside-county audience rather than every possible customer.
  2. Write service-area language that names towns and practical travel distance.
  3. Choose one visitor-facing or chamber-facing channel to test.
  4. Ask whether newsletter, event, directory, or visitor-guide placement fits.
  5. Compare response before repeating the channel.

Art opening or gallery reception

  1. Prepare artist names, image, reception time, venue, description, and sales/viewing notes.
  2. Route through gallery, tourism, arts, venue, media, and partner channels.
  3. Use the same packet for calendar submission, press note, social post, and partner email.
  4. Save screenshots, coverage, attendance, and post-event images.

Nonprofit fundraiser or public program

  1. Write the public benefit in one sentence before writing the flyer.
  2. Choose partners, calendars, referral channels, and media based on the audience served.
  3. Track listings, shares, attendance, donations, volunteer contacts, and referrals.
  4. Use those records in board reports, sponsor updates, and grant documentation.

Nonprofit proof loop

Turn visibility work into documentation.

Board members, sponsors, and grant reviewers often need evidence that a program reached the public. Save proof as part of the outreach cycle rather than trying to reconstruct it later.

ActionProof to saveLater use
Submit event to calendarConfirmation email, live link, or screenshotGrant report, board packet, sponsor update
Partner shares programPost link, newsletter screenshot, or emailCollaboration proof
Media covers eventArticle link, PDF, or photo creditPublic-benefit evidence
Public attendance happensSign-in count, photos, notes, or intake totalsImpact reporting
Referral partner respondsEmail, meeting note, or contact logPartnership development

Immediate directory

Find the right route.

Search by need, county, town, audience, or task. Results include practical links and update reminders so users can act without treating old details as final.

Results update after submitting the form or after a short pause while typing. Press Escape to close this panel.
